nodes.text: Good evening. Before you switch off, let’s take a quick look at how today went.
If you had to sum up your day in one word, what would it be?
Looking at what went well — what really made it happen?
options: I made good decisions in the moment

nodes.text: When something small went off-track, how did you respond?
When things didn’t go as expected, what was your first instinct?
options: Figure out what I could still control
Wait for someone to step in

nodes.text: Looking back now, was there a moment you had more choice than it felt like?
options: Yes, I could have acted differently
Maybe, but it wasn’t obvious then

nodes.text: There’s a pattern here — you’re not just reacting, you’re shaping outcomes. Even small adjustments like these are what build real control over time.
Days like this can feel like things just happen to you. But even noticing where you *might* have had a choice is a shift — that’s where agency starts to grow.
Let’s shift gears a bit — from what happened to you, to what you brought into the day.
Think of one interaction today. What stands out most?
options: I helped someone beyond my role
I focused on my own tasks
I felt I deserved more recognition
I felt others weren’t doing enough

if: I helped someone beyond my role
I focused on my own tasks
I felt I deserved more recognition
I felt others weren’t doing enough

nodes.text: What drove you to step in and help?
options: It felt like the right thing
I didn’t think much about it

nodes.text: Where was most of your attention during the day?
options: Finishing what I had to do

nodes.text: You stepped beyond just your own tasks. That kind of contribution often goes unnoticed in the moment — but it quietly compounds into trust and influence.
It’s natural to notice what’s missing — recognition, effort, fairness. But shifting even a little toward contribution can change how the whole system responds to you.
One last shift — let’s zoom out and look beyond just your own experience.
When you think about today’s biggest moment, who comes to mind first?
How did your actions affect others today?
options: Made things smoother for the team

nodes.text: What was taking up most of your mental space?
You weren’t just inside your own day — you were part of something larger. That shift in perspective is often where meaning starts to show up.
When everything feels personal, the weight increases. Even briefly considering others can ease that — not by ignoring your problems, but by placing them in a wider frame.
Today wasn’t just about what happened — it was about how you responded, what you contributed, and how wide your perspective was. Noticing these patterns is how better days are built.
That’s it for today. See you tomorrow.
